title: Land subsidence risk to infrastructure in US metropolises

data_scope.groundwater_wells: 90 wells across 13 cities (27 confined, 42 unconfined, 21 unknown)

core_conclusion: Sentinel-1 InSAR at ~28 m (2015-2021) shows at least 20% of every one of the 28 most populous US cities is sinking, mainly from groundwater extraction, affecting ~34 million people; subsidence is a major inland (not only coastal) hazard, and infrastructure damage is driven by differential land motion, placing more than 29,000 buildings in high/very-high damage-risk zones.
why_it_matters: Reframes subsidence as a nationwide inland hazard, isolates differential settlement (not absolute sinking) as the damage mechanism, quantifies population and building exposure, identifies confined-aquifer groundwater decline as the actionable driver (rho=0.87), and releases open high-resolution VLM and risk datasets.
why_nature: Systematic, comparable framework across 28 metropolises; couples a geophysical Earth process to urban society and infrastructure; strongly policy-translatable (zoning, codes, groundwater regulation); rigorous mm-level InSAR validated against 154 GNSS stations; open data as reusable infrastructure.
